coredata.dc:title: Novel N, F co-doped carbon dots to detect sulfide and cadmium ions with high selectivity and sensitivity based on a “turn-off-on” mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Yutian
Xu, Zhibin
Liu, Aikun
Cai, Haojie
Zhang, Mingming
Song, Jun
Zeng, Pengju
Qu, Junle
Guo, Jiaqing
Li, Hao
coredata.dc:description: In this work, novel nitrogen, fluorine co-doped carbon dots (N, F-CDs) have been synthesized by a hydrothermal method using melamine and 2, 4-difluorobenzoic acid as precursors. The obtained N, F-CDs exhibit excellent optical stability with high quantum yield. The N, F-CDs are highly selective to S2− detection by forming N, F-CDs@S2−, with a detection limit of 168 nM. The N, F-CDs@S2− were shown to be a highly selective Cd2+ detector by forming CdS, with a detection limit of 340 nM. The fluorescence quenching of N, F-CDs by S2− occurs via a static quenching mechanism and the fluorescence recovery was due to the high affinity of Cd2+ to S2−. The fluorescent sensor was used to determine Cd2+ and S2− in tap waters with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Fluorescent probe
Sulfide detection
Cadmium detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of S2− by N, F-CDs
Detection of Cd2+ by N, F-CDs@S2-
Possible quenching mechanism for detecting S2− and Cd2+
Detection of S2− and Cd2+ in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61620106016 / 61835009 / 62175161 / 61775145 ); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M702240 ); Shenzhen Basic Research Program ( JCYJ20210324095810028 / JCYJ20210324095613036 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, F-CDs. (b) Particle size distribution of N, F-CDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum of N, F-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N, F-CDs. High-resolution spectra for (b) C1s. (c) N1s. (d) O1s. (e) F1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption (blue line), maximum fluorescence excitation (black line), and maximum emission spectra (red line) of the N, F-CDs in an aqueous solution. Inset: aqueous solution of N, F-CDs under visible light (left) and 488 nm UV light (right). (b) Excitation-emission map of the N, F-CD aqueous solution in the excitation range of 300–600 nm.

(a) Normalized fluorescence intensity of N, F-CDs in various concentrations of NaCl. (b) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N, F-CDs. (c) Effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of N, F-CDs. (d) Normalized fluorescence intensity under different irradiation times.

(a) Fluorescence selectivity of N, F-CDs in the presence of various metal ions and anions at 100 μM concentration. (b) Interference of anions in sulfide ion detection (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with increasing S2− concentrations (from top to bottom: 0–70 μM). (d) Linear relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity changes and S2− concentrations (0–30 μM). (e) Reaction time with S2− addition.

(a) Fluorescence selectivity of N, F-CDs@S2− in the presence of various metal ions at 100 μM concentration (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with increasing concentration of Cd2+ (from bottom to top: 0–50 μM). (c) Linear relationship between the ratio of the fluorescence intensity change and concentrations of Cd2+ (0–30 μM). (d) Reaction time with the addition of Cd2+.

(a) UV absorptions of S2− (blue line), excitation (black line) and emission (red line) spectra of N, F-CDs. (b) UV absorptions of N, F-CDs and N, F-CDs@ S2−. (c) Fluorescence lifetime decay spectra of N, F-CDs with increasing concentration of S2− (0, 30, 40 μM). (d) Emission spectra of N, F-CDs (red line), N, F-CDs + S2− (blue line), and N, F-CDs + S2− + Cd2+ (yellow line).
Synthesis of N, F-CDs and their application.
Comparison of different methods for determining S2−.
Comparison of different methods for determining Cd2+.
Determination of S2− in water samples (n = 3, 95% confidence level).
Determination of Cd2+ in water samples (n = 3, 95% confidence level).

head.ce:title: Novel N, F co-doped carbon dots to detect sulfide and cadmium ions with high selectivity and sensitivity based on a “turn-off-on” mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Biomedical Optics and Photonics (CBOP) & College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Lab of Optoelectronics Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Biomedical Optics and Photonics (CBOP) & College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering
Key Lab of Optoelectronics Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Biomedical Optics and Photonics (CBOP) & College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Lab of Optoelectronics Devices and systems of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Research Nuclear University MEPhI (Moscow Engineering Physics Institute), 115409, Moscow, Russian Federation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Research Nuclear University MEPhI (Moscow Engineering Physics Institute)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Research Nuclear University MEPhI (Moscow Engineering Physics Institute), 115409, Moscow, Russian Federation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, novel nitrogen, fluorine co-doped carbon dots (N, F-CDs) have been synthesized by a hydrothermal method using melamine and 2, 4-difluorobenzoic acid as precursors. The obtained N, F-CDs exhibit excellent optical stability with high quantum yield. The N, F-CDs are highly selective to S2− detection by forming N, F-CDs@S2−, with a detection limit of 168 nM. The N, F-CDs@S2− were shown to be a highly selective Cd2+ detector by forming CdS, with a detection limit of 340 nM. The fluorescence quenching of N, F-CDs by S2− occurs via a static quenching mechanism and the fluorescence recovery was due to the high affinity of Cd2+ to S2−. The fluorescent sensor was used to determine Cd2+ and S2− in tap waters with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial wastewater has always been a focus area, because the accumulation of inorganic and heavy-metal pollutants in living organisms causes diseases and inhibits functions and mechanisms in living systems [1–3].

Sulfur is an example of an inorganic chemical widely used in the industry [4], whose ion (sulfide, S2−) is a major byproduct of dyeing, agriculture, paper-making, and sewage processes [5]. In the environment, S2− is a pollutant that forms acidic hydrogen sulfide, which is toxic to the human body [6,7]; many diseases have been associated with the prevalence of high S2− concentrations in air and water, including Down's syndrome [8], Alzheimer's disease [9], and diabetes [10].

Similarly, cadmium is an example of a heavy metal, whose Cd2+ ions are widely used in battery manufacturing, pigments, and other chemical industries [11–13]. Also considered to be toxic, the presence of Cd2+ in air and water causes serious harm to the human body [14,15]; health effects such as acute respiratory failure, heart diseases, emphysema, and cancer have been related to the slow metabolism of Cd2+ in the human body [16–18].

The above examples illustrate the importance of the quantitative detection of S2− and Cd2+ in water; studies have used techniques such as chromatography, titration, colorimetry, spectroscopy, and electrochemistry [12,19–21]. However, these methods require the complicated processing of test samples and offer limited selectivity and sensitivity. To this end, fluorescence analysis using carbon dots (CDs) is promising to overcome these shortcomings. As a novel nanomaterial, carbon dots have been intensively studied over the past two decades due to its outstanding properties such as low cost, low toxicity, superior water solubility, and high chemical stability [22–26]. Besides these features, CDs high offer a chemical sensitivity and a tunable selectivity, which brings the possibility for a wide range of applications [27–29]. In recent years, CDs have been widely used in various fields, such as ion detection, bioimaging, sensors, catalysis, and biomedicine [30]. Though a wide variety of carbon dots have been used to detect S2− and Cd2+, there have been no reports of carbon dots that can detect both ions sensitively and selectively.

Here, novel nitrogen, fluorine co-doped carbon dots (N, F-CDs) with high quantum yields were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using melamine and 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid as precursors (Scheme 1 ). The optical properties of N, F-CDs and sensing behavior for S2− and Cd2+ detections were evaluated, which demonstrated the potential application of this system by characterizing real water samples.

Melamine, 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid, sodium sulfide, ethanol, and a variety of metal salts and sodium salts (MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, LiCl, CdCl2·2H2O, ZnCl2, CuSO4, CsBr, (CH3COO)2Pb, KI, (CH3COO)2Sr, (CH3COO)2Ba, NaCl, NaF, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaNO3, NaNO2, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, and NaH2PO2) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without additional treatment. Ultrapure water was used as the solvent for all the solutions.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 F20, USA) was employed to determine the N, F-CD morphology, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi, USA) to determine the chemical composition of the N, F-CDs, and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Nicolet 5700 spectrometer, USA) to determine the chemical structures of the N, F-CDs.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (UV-2550 Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) and fluorescence spectroscopy were performed, and acquired fluorescence lifetime decay spectra (Fluorolog@-3 steady-state spectrofluorometer, HORIBA Scientific) to investigate the optical properties and quantum yield of the N, F-CDs.

The N, F-CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using melamine and 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid as precursors. Here, 2, 4-difluorobenzoic acid (6.324 g, 0.04 mol), melamine (0.126 g, 0.001 mol), ethanol (1 mL) and ultrapure water (20 mL) were transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. The reaction product was allowed to cool to room temperature (25 °C), yielding the N, F-CD solution. The supernatant of the solutions was filtered using a 0.22 μm micropore film filter and then diluted ten times for further experiments. For the later weighting and characterization of the N, F-CDs, the filtered solution was freeze-dried after dialysis against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane for 1 day to remove impurities. The weight of the N, F-CDs was determined to be 8.2 mg mL−1.

To determine the selectivity of the N, F-CDs for detecting S2−, each test solution was prepared by mixing 2400 μL of deionized water, 300 μL of the N, F-CDs (0.82 mg mL−1) solutions, and 300 μL of the ions of interest (1 mM) in a 4.5 mL quartz cuvette. After 10 min of reaction, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded from 499 to 600 nm at 495 nm excitation. All experiments were repeated at least three times.

To determine the sensitivity of the N, F-CDs for detecting S2−, each test solution was prepared by mixing 300 μL of the N, F-CD solution (0.82 mg mL−1), 2400 μL of ultrapure water, and 300 μL of a S2− solution of different concentrations in a 4.5 mL cuvette. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at 495 nm excitation for plotting the standard S2− detection curve. All experiments were repeated at least three times.

For this, 300 μL of the N, F-CD solution (0.82 mg mL−1), 2400 μL of ultrapure water, and 120 μL of S2− ions (1 mM) were mixed, added to a 4.5 mL cuvette, and reacted for 5 min to form the CDs@S2− sensing platform. Subsequently, 180 μL of different concentrations of Cd2+ solutions were added to the abovementioned mixture at room temperature and allowed to stand for 10 min to complete the reaction. The selectivity of the N, F-CDs@S2− nanocomplex toward Cd2+ was investigated by adding various metal ions (Na+, K+, Li+, Cs+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Cu2+,Zn2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, and Ba2+) to the detection system. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded until the reaction was complete.

Tap water was used as the real sample, which was first filtered through a 0.22 μm PES water-phase membrane. The filtered water samples were then mixed with S2− and Cd2+ solutions to obtain different concentrations (0.5 μM, 1, and 2 μM), following which the N, F-CDs, and N, F-CDs@S2− were added to these solutions. The contents of S2− and Cd2+ in the solutions were quantified by the fluorescence intensity. All experiments were performed three times.

The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) shows the morphology, particle size, and dispersion of the synthesized N, F-CDs, which are mostly spherical and uniformly dispersed. The size distribution of the N, F-CDs ranged from 1 to 5.5 nm with an average diameter of 2.91 nm (Fig. 1b).

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1c) shows the surface functional groups of the N, F-CDs, confirming the incorporation of different elements into the CDs. The broad absorption peak at 3120 cm−1 is assigned to O–H and N–H stretching; the peak at 1609 cm−1 to CO stretching; the peak at 1396 cm−1 to C–N stretching; the peaks at 1271 cm−1 and 971 cm−1 to covalent C–F stretching and C–F stretching, respectively, indicating the incorporation of the N, F-CDs; and the peak at 779 cm−1 to aromatic stretching.

XPS was performed to confirm the FT-IR results on the N, F-CD surface functional groups. The full XPS profile of the N, F-CDs (Fig. 2 a) shows four peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and F1s, respectively. The composition of the N, F-CDs was 52.9% carbon, 13.2% nitrogen, 22.1% oxygen, and 11.8% fluorine. The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2b) show four peaks at 284.8, 286.2, 287.1, and 289.1 eV, assigned to the C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, CO, and C–F groups, respectively.

The high-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. 2c) show three peaks at 399, 400.05, and 401.3 eV, attributed to pyridinic N, amino N, and graphitic N, respectively [31]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows three peaks at 531.2, 532, and 533.3 eV, attributed to CO, C–O, and OC–O groups, respectively. The high-resolution F1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) shows two peaks at 685 and 687.2 eV, assigned to C–F and covalent C–F groups, respectively [32,33].

These results confirm both, the incorporation of N and F into the N, F-CD structure and the functionalization of the N, F-CD surfaces by various groups that are available for reaction with the ions of interest.

Acquired UV–vis and fluorescence spectra to determine the optical properties of the N, F-CDs. The UV–vis spectrum of the N, F-CDs (blue curve, Fig. 3 a) shows two absorption peaks at 220 nm and 264 nm corresponding to the n-π* transition of the CO bond and π–π* transition of the CC bond, respectively [34]. The maximum emission wavelength (red line in Fig. 3a) is at 512 nm under a maximum excitation wavelength of 495 nm (black line in Fig. 3a). The N, F-CD aqueous solution is a clear, pale yellow liquid (inset, Fig. 3a) in visible light (left picture) and shows strong green-light emission under UV irradiation at 488 nm (right picture).

Fig. 3b and Fig. S1 shows the excitation-emission map of the N, F-CD aqueous solution in the excitation range of 300–600 nm. The emission wavelength does not change with the excitation wavelength, which is a typical excitation-independent fluorescence phenomenon. The fluorescence quantum yield of the as-prepared N, F-CDs at room temperature is 65.50%.

The chemical and optical stabilities of the N, F-CDs were investigated in different environments, in terms of the ionic strength, pH, temperature, and irradiation time [35–37]. Fig. 4 a compares the N, F-CD fluorescence intensities at different NaCl concentrations, showing no significant change in strong fluorescence. This indicates that the N, F-CDs are stable even in a high-ionic-strength salt solution.

Fig. 4b compares the N, F-CD fluorescence intensities at different pH levels, showing steady, strong fluorescence in strongly acidic (pH = 1.0–6.0) and neutral conditions (pH = 6.0–9.0); a slight fluorescence reduction in weakly alkaline conditions (pH = 10.0–11.0); and almost no fluorescence in strongly alkaline conditions (pH = 12.0–14.0). Thus, the N, F-CDs are potentially suitable for ion detection in biological systems and bioimaging.

Fig. 4c compares the N, F-CD fluorescence intensities at different temperatures from 30 to 80 °C, showing a slight intensity decrease within this temperature range. Fig. 4d compares the N, F-CD fluorescence intensities under 1 h of UV light exposure, showing no significant change throughout the exposure time. The foregoing initial tests of the N, F-CDs are indicative of good temperature-stability and photo-stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S2− by N, F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the N, F-CDs for detecting S2− were tested by independently measuring the fluorescence responses of the N, F-CDs for the following ions: I−, Cl−, Ac−, Br−, NO2 −, HCO3 −, F−, H2PO4 −, H2PO2 −, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, Na+, K+, Li+, Cs+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, and Cd2+. Fig. 5 a shows that only the presence of S2− significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity. Further, Fig. 5b shows that the addition of the aforementioned test anions to the S2--quenched N, F-CD solution has virtually no effect on the quenched fluorescence, indicating that the test anions do not interfere with S2− detection by N, F-CDs.

Fig. 5c demonstrates the effect of the S2− concentration on the N, F-CD fluorescence intensity; increasing the S2− concentration from 0 to 70 μM causes not only a decrease in the fluorescence intensity at the 512 nm emission peak, but also a red shift of the 512 nm emission peak to 520 nm at S2− concentrations of 40 μM and higher. These results suggest that the quenching mechanism involves S2− reacting with the surface functional groups of the N, F-CDs to form N, F-CDs@S2−.

Fig. 5d shows the linear relationship between the ratio of the fluorescence intensity change (F0–F)/F0 and S2− concentration from 0 to 30 μM (F and F0 represent the intensities of the N, F-CDs with and without S2−, respectively). The R2 coefficient of 0.99658 estimated by linear fitting confirms the good linear fit. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 168 nM using equation 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of 11 blank measurements and k is the slope of the linear calibration plot.

Fig. 5e shows the time evolution of the quenching of the N, F-CD fluorescence intensity by S2− ions; 1.5 min after adding S2−, the fluorescence intensity rapidly decreases and remains stable for the remaining 10 min. Thus, the N, F-CD system is a reliable and sensitive sensor of S2− ions. Table 1 compares this work to previously reported systems, showing that the N, F-CD system has both, a larger linear range and lower detection limit. The N, F-CDs not only can detect S2− anions directly, but are also highly selective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cd2+ by N, F-CDs@S2-
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence recovery properties of the N, F-CDs@S2− sensor was tested according to the “turn-off-on” mechanism. Fig. 6 a compares the effects of adding individual metal ions to the N, F-CDs@S2− solution on the fluorescence of the sensor. While the fluorescence intensity is recovered upon adding 100 μM Cd2+, the other metal ions show no significant effect on the quenched fluorescence, indicating the selectivity of N, F-CDs@S2− for Cd2+.

Fig. 6b demonstrates the effect of the Cd2+ concentration on the N, F-CDs@S2− fluorescence intensity; increasing the Cd2+ concentration from 0 to 50 μM causes an increase in the fluorescence intensity at the 512 nm emission peak.

Fig. 6c shows the linear relationship between the ratio of the fluorescence intensity change (F–F0)/F0 and concentration of Cd2+ from 0 to 30 μM (F and F0 represent the intensities of N, F-CDs@S2− with and without Cd2+, respectively). The R2 coefficient of 0.99347 estimated by linear fitting confirms the good linear fit. The LOD was calculated to be 340 nM using the equation 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of 11 blank measurements and k is the slope of the linear calibration plot

Fig. 6d shows the time evolution of the recovery of the fluorescence intensity by Cd2+ ions; fluorescence recovery is achieved 10 min after adding Cd2+, and remains stable for the remaining 25 min. These results indicate the rapid and stable fluorescence recovery of N, F-CDs@S2− by Cd2+ ions. Table 2 compares this work to previously reported systems, showing that the N, F-CDs@S2− system has both, a larger linear range and a lower detection limit. The N, F-CDs can not only detect Cd2+ anions sensitively, but are also highly selective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism for detecting S2− and Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching can occur through different mechanisms including the inner filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), static quenching, and dynamic quenching. To determine the quenching mechanism of N, F-CDs in the “turn-off-on” detection of S2− and Cd2+, the optical properties of the detection system were further investigated.

First, analyzed the data for the IFE, FRET, and PET. Fig. 7 a shows the UV absorption spectra of the S2− solution (blue curve) with the excitation and emission spectra of the N, F-CDs (black curve). The fact that the UV absorption of S2− does not overlap with the excitation or emission spectrum of the N, F-CDs excludes the possibility of the IFE, FRET, and PET being the mechanisms for the fluorescence quenching of N, F-CDs by S2−.

Next, the static and the dynamic quenching mechanisms were analyzed. In general, while static quenching characteristically causes changes in the UV–vis absorption spectrum due to the formation of ground-state complexes between the quencher and the N, F-CDs, dynamic quenching causes no such change. Fig. 7b compares the UV absorptions of N, F-CDs and N, F-CDs@S2−, showing that the maximum absorption peak of the N, F-CDs at 220 nm shifts to 217 nm upon adding S2−. This suggests that the N, F-CDs, and S2− react to form a ground-state nanocomplex and causes the change of UV–vis absorption spectrum of N, F-CDs, which is the typical static quenching phenomenon.

To further verify the quenching mechanism of the N, F-CDs by S2−, the fluorescence lifetime decay spectra of the N, F-CDs with increasing S2− concentrations of 0, 30, and 40 μM were examined. As Fig. 7c indicates, the fluorescence decay spectra for all three concentrations can be fitted with a single-exponential curve. Further, the S2− concentrations of 0, 30, and 40 μM show no significant change in the fluorescence lifetimes, which are measured to be 2.98, 3.00, and 3.01 ns, respectively. This result further indicates that the quenching mechanism for detecting S2− will not be dynamic quenching or FRET, which will reduce the fluorescence lifetime.

Fig. 7d compares the emission spectra of N, F-CDs, N, F-CDs@S2−, and N, F-CDs@S2− + Cd2+, indicating that with S2− addition, the maximum emission at 512 nm red-shifts to 520 nm, and with Cd2+ addition, the maximum emission returns to 512 nm. Fig. 7c and d indicate that the quenching mechanism of S2− is most likely static rather than dynamic. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of the N, F-CDs by S2− occurs via static quenching.

According to previous reports [47], applying the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal equation to the N, F-CDs@S2−, the association constant of the nanocomplex could be calculated to be 2.91 × 105 L mol −1. Based on the competitive binding mechanism, the fluorescence recovery is explained by the formation of CdS. The solubility product constant of CdS is 8.0 × 10−27 [48], which is much stronger than the association constant of 2.91 × 105 between N, F-CDs and S2−. When the Cd2+ was added to N, F-CDs@S2− sensing system, more stable than CdS was formed due to the high affinity of Cd2+ for S2− resulting in the fluorescence recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S2− and Cd2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: With rapid industrial development, sulfide and cadmium may exist in our water system; therefore, it is necessary to evaluate the accuracy and reliability of fluorescent sensors in real samples (tap water from Shenzhen, China). The spike recovery method was used to verify the accuracy of the proposed sensor.

Table 3 shows that with the addition of S2− standard solutions at different concentrations, the measured spiked recoveries in tap water range from 96.2% to 103.5%, indicating the good recovery rates of S2− in real samples.

Table 4 shows that with the addition of Cd2+ standard solutions at different concentrations, the measured spiked recoveries in tap water range from 97.8% to 103.6%, indicating the good recovery rates of Cd2+ in real samples. Thus, the fluorescent probes can quantitatively measure S2− and Cd2+, indicating the reliability of N, F-CDs and the potential for actual detection in real samples.

A hydrothermal method was used to synthesize environmentally friendly green N, F-CDs using melamine and 2,4-difluorobenzoic as precursors. The as-prepared N, F-CDs offered a high quantum yield compared to other reported N, F-CDs, and also possessed numerous surface functional groups that could be employed for S2− ion detection via the formation of N, F-CDs@S2−. Unlike most previously reported studies on S2− detection by CDs, the detection mechanism involves static quenching, where S2− ions directly quench the fluorescence of N, F-CDs to achieve quantitative detection with a linear range from 0 to 30 μM and a detection limit of 168 nM. Further, based on the affinity of Cd2+ for S2−, the fluorescence recovery of the N, F-CDs@S2− by Cd2+ was used to detect Cd2+, achieving a detection limit of 340 nM. The combined “turn-off-on” detection method was used to measure the S2− and Cd2+ contents in real water samples and demonstrated recoveries of 96.2%–103.6% and 97.8%–103.5%, respectively. The results of this study indicate that our fluorescence “turn-off-on” sensor is highly promising for monitoring sulfide and cadmium in water and biological systems.

Yutian Zeng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zhibin Xu: Methodology. Aikun Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Haojie Cai: Validation, Investigation. Mingming Zhang: Validation, Resources. Jun Song: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Pengju Zeng: Investigation, Resources. Junle Qu: Funding acquisition. Jiaqing Guo: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Hao Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (61620106016/61835009/62175161/61775145); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M702240); Shenzhen Basic Research Program (JCYJ20210324095810028/JCYJ20210324095613036).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110379.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent and colorimetric assay for determination of Cu(II) and Hg(II) using AuNPs reduced and wrapped by carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li YX, Tang L, Zhu CX, Liu XY, Wang X, Liu Y. Fluorescent and colorimetric assay for determination of Cu(II) and Hg(II) using AuNPs reduced and wrapped by carbon dots. Microchim Acta. 2022;189(1).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Principles, mechanisms, and application of carbon quantum dots in sensors: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Molaei MJ. Principles, mechanisms, and application of carbon quantum dots in sensors: a review. Anal Methods-Uk. 2020;12(10):1266-1287.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of carbon dots and their composite materials for the detection and removal of radioactive ions: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang Z, Zhang LY, Zhang KJ, Lu YX, Chen J, Wang SQ, et al. Application of carbon dots and their composite materials for the detection and removal of radioactive ions: A review. Chemosphere. 2022;287.
